sentences.text: OK.
Mm-hmm.
I really don't remember too much about it except going into it.
It was nothing like Ohrdruf.
I mean, the people looked fairly healthy.
They seemed happy to see us.
Of course, we didn't speak the, you know-- very few guys spoke any French.
And-- but that's, that's about all I remember now.
But there-- but we had run into something like that before.
So that kind of gave us a-- at least me-- a little idea what was going on.
And then, when we ran into Ohrdruf, it was completely different, you know, to see something way different.
Yeah, a lot of people on this-- on the roads coming back, going back towards our lines because we-- most of the mains cities we hit pretty hard ahead of us.
And if there was any problem with German troops, especially the SS, our, our artillery would just-- would just pound the dickens out of them before, you know-- before the attack.
And so if they were in town, the towns got it.
And plus the towns were bombed quite heavy, too.
Any, any town of any size was bombed quite heavily.
So the people had to go somewhere.
And they couldn't-- the Germans weren't-- wouldn't let them go east.
And so they just more or less went west because we just let them go through.
No.
We were moving so fast that, I mean, you didn't even have time to, to really think.
I don't know.
I, I, I, I couldn't say for sure.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of-- I, I didn't run into him personally, but one of my buddies [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was Jewish.
And, and he spoke German and Yiddish.
She was-- and they-- you know, she had all the papers and everything.
But she actually was Jewish.
And someone had got her, one of the churches or-- the-- a lot of churches gave them baptismal certificates to say that they were Christian.
And that's-- a lot of people-- most of them, you couldn't tell by their name anyway.
But she was Jewish.
And she wanted to talk to an American Jew.
And that's-- this, this one guy they brought, brought in.
But that's, that's the only one that I can remember really anything-- had any direct contact.
And most of them, by that time, were either in Poland at Auschwitz or gone, I mean, or in the camps-- hadn't been released or anything.
And no camp had been released yet other than this-- the one that we-- that I know of is the French.
And there were no Jews in there.
It was strictly French POWs.
It was like you know, or whatever it is.
I-- from what I hear, she, she just-- she just broke down.
I mean, she, she was holding it in for so long.
She had to talk to someone and, you know, and get-- because all, all she had to have was one person say she's not a Christian.
She's a Jew.
And she would've been gone.
[LAUGHS]
So I-- it was probably real hard for her.
So by talking-- to finding someone like that, then he could-- then, of course, he, he turned her over to the American authorities, you know.
And they moved her out and stuff.
But other than that, I don't know of any other case personally that I know of a person that had any contact up until we actually went into Ohrdruf.
How did the American GI react to this encounter?
With Ohrdruf?
No.
With, with the young girl.
[SIGHS] [CRYING] It-- their, their hearts just went out to her.
I mean, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, she was telling us things that, that we didn't know.
And it's-- I think, though, still there was at-- in the back of our minds, things like that aren't happening.
It can't happen.
This is a civilized country.
You just don't do things like that, even though you got someone right there telling you that it does.
I, I don't know.
From-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never talked to her personally.
I'm just telling you what, what I heard, you know, what he told afterwards.
But evidently, she lost most of her family.
At that time, she just thought they were separated.
And she hoped to find them.
We were probably, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say, 75 to 80 miles almost due south of Berlin.
We could've gone in within less than two weeks--
--at the rate we were going.
Yeah.
Actually, we weren't looking for the-- it was the, the 6th Armored, I think it was-- had, had word.
We were already passed it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we, we were way ahead of anybody else.
We were all-- way out on spearhead all by ourselves.
So we came back.
And they came-- they came forward.
And we came back.
Well, they just missed Rundstedt-- von Rundstedt by one day, we hear.
He was, was the big German general--
--one of the number one guys.

And from what we hear now, I think it was Goebbels was preparing a, a big sweep for Hitler for his birthday-- which was in April, middle of April sometime, I think it was-- in this underground communications center, big elaborate sweep.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, they went in, in the town.
And this one particular time, we were-- it was-- it's a maneuver that's common.
We, we, we were doing it all the time.
One division would go ahead.
And we would fall back.
Then, then they would fall back.
And we would go ahead.
We were leapfrogging instead of just pushing.
I mean, it was kind of-- and so we, we came.
The 6th Armored was coming east.
And we were coming southwest and crossed over.
And we were going to lay it-- a wire from our new headquarters-- back.
And we only had one wire crew at headquarters.
We just laid the one wire from headquarters to headquarters.
And everybody else laid wires to us.
And division laid wires to us, division did.
And it just happened to come across this camp.
The way I remember it was almost a solid wall of buildings along a road, very near to a road.
And there was a, a gate.
And as we were approaching, there were other GIs in the-- in the vicinity.
And there was some gunfire.
And and there were two German guards at the gate.
And they were shot and killed.
And by time we got there, there was quite a commotion and people running around almost like chickens with their heads chopped off.
Nobody knew what to do.
All GIs.
We went into the camp.
And that's, that's when we saw it.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Now, I don't remember if there was a Arbeit macht frei on the-- over the gate that's over all the camps-- that they say in Germany, work will make you free.
I don't remember if that was there.
All I remember is the two-- I'm-- I-- positive there was two, two dead Germans laying there.
And then when we went in, and this-- the first thing, more or less, we saw was this-- like, a courtyard and all these dead bodies laying in kind of, more or less, a group.
And I went back out to our wire truck.
And I got my camera.
It was a little Baby Brownie, plastic Baby Brownie.
It took 128 film, which is probably about the size of a 35 millimeter today.
And I had one picture left on the roll.
And that's all the film I had.
And that was the first thing I saw.
So that was-- I took that picture.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that picture, even though it was the first thing I saw, was quite a famous picture because that is the exact same picture that was shown all over the world of Eisenhower and Patton and all his entourage viewing the bodies, these bodies laying there.
Except my picture didn't have any of the, the generals standing around looking.
I always tell the people when I talk to them when I give a lecture on it, my picture is showing what they saw.
And the other picture shows who saw it.
But then we start walking, you know.
There weren't very many [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prisoners alive.

These were all-- had been shot just hours before the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- [CRYING] they had taken all the prisoners who could walk and marched them out and-- because they knew-- they knew we were coming.
And these people were-- probably were the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] weren't strong enough or something.
And they just shot them there.
And then the ones that were still alive were just scattered throughout, you know, in the camp in various places.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were absolutely [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just walking skeletons.
That's all they were, no expression on their face, blank eyes.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] Oh, god.
[CRYING] See, the worst part is I, I see all this stuff.
I, [SOBBING] I can't get it out of my mind.
[SIGHS] Most of them, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very few of them, maybe one or two of them could talk.
They were-- they were just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know if they knew who we were or not.
They, they didn't show any emotion to, to what I could see-- maybe afterwards when they, you know, realized what was going on.
But [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] oh, man, it-- absolutely no expression, just gone.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I, I don't know who was in more shock, the prisoners or us.
I think most of the-- at-- the first few minutes, hour or so, everybody was just-- just seemed to be just wandering around, looking.
A couple, couple inmates did come and kind of show us around.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We went into some of the barracks.
There were dead bodies all in there.
From what I understand now, when a person died, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the inmates had to dispose of the bodies.
And when, when the people became so weak that they couldn't, the bodies just laid there.
So you'd see a-- maybe a living person, a living corpse laying there and right next to them, maybe four or five dead ones.
You couldn't tell one from the other, except the one moved a little bit.
We, we saw one room that bodies were just piled up like cordwood.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think that probably was the room that-- where Patton got sick.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because it-- I mean, I'm telling this from what I hear, that when he saw this one room piled up with bodies, he, he got sick to his stomach and had to leave.
And I think this was probably the room that he was talking about.
There's a picture of it there.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we, we found the, the mass graves, just great big, huge holes just scooped out of the earth with bulldozers where the bodies were thrown in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I talk to the kids or anybody, I tell them-- give you some idea what it looked like.
Just take a piece of paper, take a pencil, and start making little [POINTING] marks all over.

And when you get the whole paper all covered, that's a diagram of what, what these graves looked like with the bodies just thrown in all over, arms sticking up, legs sticking up, butts sticking up, heads sticking up-- all over.
Most of them naked-- some of them had clothes on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were evidence that they were trying to burn the bodies.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You, you got to remember, we're going into this place cold and not knowing anything, what is-- what's going on anywhere.
So some of this what I'm telling, I found out afterwards and relating it to what we saw.

But evidently, even though this was just a labor camp where they were systematically starving the people to death-- which is a, a more horrible form of death than putting them in a gas chamber like they did in Auschwitz-- that they were trying to hide some of these bodies.
They were actually digging up, pulling bodies out of these mass graves and trying to burn them before the, the Americans and-- or whoever got there, got there.
There were signs.
They had brought ovens and stuff in.
Some places, there were just piles of bodies, like on railroad ties-- not ties, no, the rails, iron rails, make, like, a grill and piled the bodies up on that and burning them that way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were-- there were-- anywhere you went, there were bodies all over.
There were some [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] buildings that they were, like, using them for-- they weren't really barracks because they weren't that type of building.
It was more like a, a cow barn with a big door on one end and a big door on the other and with an aisle down the center.
And most of them had bunks in them.
And we went through them.
And there weren't too many living people left.
But the ones that were laying in their bunks, then they, they, they just stare at you.
[MOANS] It's-- it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's hard to describe.
That's why I think the pictures and stuff are so important.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were a lot of dead bodies in there that, you know, they had taken out.
So maybe some of these dead-- or the ones that were still alive they thought were dead and just left them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One, one barn didn't have any bunks at all in it.
It was just empty.
And they were just laying around, bodies laying around.
But over in a corner, this, this one man, this one living skeleton, I'll, I'll, I'll never forget him.
He was sitting in the-- up against the wall.
And his feet were pulled up like this.
And he was just, just absolutely terrified.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He just-- he just sat there, never moved.
He was alive.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] these people, most of them, were to a point where [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if you just said, [SIGHS], they would have died right there.
I mean, they were that close to death.
So you don't know what they were thinking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I never think about the other ones that were still alive.
But I always-- that one there, even to this day, I wonder, wonder if he made it.
One, one, one thing I'm really thankful for-- I never thought about it at the time.
I was always sorry we didn't.
But we didn't have any food with us.
At least we-- my group didn't.
There were just three or four of us in my party.
We didn't have any water with us because we, we were expecting to go to this next town, lay the wire back, and we'd be back at our headquarters.
So we didn't have anything to give these people.
And the blessing is that we didn't because we know now that it would have killed them instantly.
A good drink of water or-- and our food that we had, you know, K-rations and stuff like that, was all highly concentrated.
And it would have killed them right on the spot.
They, they couldn't have handled it.
All, all they could handle was maybe some hot water with a chicken fanned over the top of it.
That's about as strong as they could as-- they could.
And Ohrdruf, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] even though it was a small camp, they don't know too much about it because when the Germans left, they took all their records.
And we never did find them.
They think there's probably-- maybe 7,000 in the camp at one time.
And out of anybody was left, there was less than 75 that we found alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I doubt if 25 survived out of the 75 because they were too far gone.
I mean, you can go to a certain point.
And it's just more or less a, a point of no return on, on that type of starvation, I think.
So many of them died.
And we-- our-- I know our medics, when they came in, had no experience on stuff like that.
And I, I, I can just imagine them trying to give them something to eat, which was the worst thing they could have done.
So I-- that's one thing I always hope that at least some of them, you know, survived because [LAUGHS] we were dumb about such things like that.
We didn't know what to do.
This is the first camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we came into.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I really-- at the time, I don't-- I don't think we fully appreciate-- I hate to say appreciate-- or fully realized what we were walking into, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if you know what I mean.
To see something like this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had never seen human beings in that condition.
We had just been through a POW camp.
And everybody looked halfway decent.
I mean, they weren't fat.
But they were alive and at least semi-healthy.

But to walk into a place like that, where the people should have been dead months before, I, I don't think [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for a long-- for maybe hours that people, the GIs, really realized what, what they had walked into, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe until the news of it got to the headquarters where, where they, they knew what was going on.
We, we didn't-- I mean, the ordinary GI doesn't know what's going on.
We didn't know from one, one town to the next where we were or where we were going or what we were doing or what we were supposed to do.
We just did it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We've got to stop now.
